title: Climate inaction to force up rates: Kean
full_text:
Natural disasters fuelled by a failure to curb global warming will make higher interest rates a permanent feature of Australia’s economy, the government’s climate change tsar Matt Kean has claimed, as analysis shows the extent to which climate inaction will harm the nation – in particular in NSW and Queensland – and reduce households’ income.
Six months after the Liberal Party formally dropped its commitment to net zero by 2050, Mr Kean, a former NSW Liberal treasurer, declared: “This report says you can’t be a serious economic manager in 2026 without being a climate realist.” The Australian Prudential Regulatory Authority has relied on economic modelling by Oxford Economics Australia to inform its first stress test of how a changing climate could affect home insurance, with the data showing the economy fares better – albeit still with challenges – when strong climate action is delayed and taken in the 2030s rather than not at all.
Mr Kean said Australia’s prudential supervisor had put a scenario estimate on the cost of climate inaction that dwarfed the cost of acting.
“We’re talking about household incomes potentially 20 per cent lower than the status quo, persistent inflation, and rising interest rates driven not by policy mistakes but by floods, fires and other disasters made worse by climate change,” Mr Kean said.
“Australians are furious about high interest rates and inflation right now, and this report tells us that without serious climate action, elevated rates and prices become a feature of our economy, not a temporary problem we can fix with a budget.
“We went to extraordinary lengths to protect household incomes in Covid. This report says climate inaction delivers a hit that’s bigger, slower, and permanent. The question that has to be asked is where’s the urgency (in acting)?” Under a delayed transition scenario, which assumes global warming is limited to 2C by 2050 after measures to mitigate emissions are introduced in the 2030s, Australia’s economy deteriorates initially as emissions prices increase but gradually recovers over time. Inflation rises from 2.6 per cent in 2030 to 3.8 per cent in 2035 but falls back to 2.9 per cent in 2040 and 2.5 per cent by the mid-century as households and businesses adapt.
The growth of households’ disposable income falls in the 2030s but then slowly recovers in the 2040s as the economy transitions away from emissions-intensive activity.
GDP growth also drops between 2030 and 2035 from 2.9 per cent to 1.5 per cent, when it then stagnates through until 2050.
Interest rates increase from 2.6 per cent in 2030 to 3.45 per cent in the middle of the century but they are 2 per cent higher under a scenario that assumes there are no additional policies to cut emissions beyond what is now implemented.
The current policies or no more action scenario assumes global warming reaches 2.5C by 2030.
Interest rates rise from 3.34 per cent in 2030 to 5.45 per cent in 2050 and inflation is also higher by 2050 – at 3 per cent when there is no action, compared to 2.5 per cent when action is delayed.
The growth of household income is higher in 2030 if no further climate action is taken compared to delayed action, but then it slows dramatically to 0.7 per cent and 0.6 per cent in the 2040s. GDP growth follows a similar pattern and is lower when there is no action than a delayed transition.
Residents of Queensland and NSW are the worst affected, with household income facing the biggest pressures when no measures are introduced to combat climate change. Household incomes still face pressure under a delayed transition, but only by about half as much. Growth in gross state product also reveals delayed action is better than no action.
The forecasts rely on global scenarios and emissions trajectories from the Network for Greening Financial Services, which is a group of central banks and supervisors established in 2017 to meet the goals of the Paris agreement.
They are designed to be stressful, to help understand what the impact on households might be, and do not reflect Labor or Coalition policies. APRA stresses NGFS scenarios should not be interpreted as official forecasts.
Nationals leader Matt Canavan said the predictions of Mr Kean “and his friends” of how net zero would lower energy prices and interest rates had “proven to be hopelessly wrong”.
“Why would this modelling be any different? In truth, the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change reports show that there is no strong link between natural disasters and carbon emissions. We should stop scaremongering about global emissions and start focusing on making our own economy stronger,” he said.
Mr Kean said the question for his Liberal colleagues and all political parties wasn’t whether to act but whether to lead and manage the transition or “get dragged through it not on our terms”.
“This is not a report about the environment, it’s a report about the Australian economy, households’ budgets and financial stability commissioned by our own financial regulators. Anyone who reads it and concludes inaction is the safe conservative choice has misread both the report and what conservatism means.” Erwin Jackson, head of Australian programs at the Climateworks Centre at Monash University, said the debate on the economy overlooked a critical risk to the nation’s productive capacity – climate change and nature loss.
“If we don’t limit global warming … the cost of recovering from extreme weather events will leave us with less money to invest in communities, schools, healthcare and infrastructure,” he said.
summary: Matt Kean, Australia's climate change tsar, warns that failing to address climate change will lead to permanently higher interest rates and lower household incomes. A report shows that the economic impact of climate inaction will be severe, particularly in NSW and Queensland.
author: ROSIE LEWIS, NATIONAL AFFAIRS CORRE
heading: General Insurance
key_points: Interest rates could rise significantly due to climate inaction.
Household incomes may drop by 20% without climate action.
Inflation is expected to increase if no measures are taken.
Queensland and NSW will be the hardest hit regions.
The report emphasizes the economic risks of ignoring climate change.
ticker_line: Climate inaction could raise interest rates permanently
